nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still surface waters: rice paddies, ponds, irrigation ditches, swamps, streams, coastal estuaries and brackish tidal creeks; heavily vegetated areas near the water surface

decorPreferences: floating plants (water lettuce, frogbit, Amazon frogbit) for surface cover and egg-laying
dense emergent or surface vegetation to reduce skittishness
subdued lighting or surface shade to reduce stress
gentle filtration (sponge filter or low-flow HOB) to avoid surface turbulence

diet.notes: Obligate surface predator. Primarily insectivorous in the wild — takes floating insects, mosquito larvae, small invertebrates, and zooplankton at the water surface. In the aquarium, readily accepts high-quality floating flake, freeze-dried tubifex, and frozen/live foods: bloodworm, daphnia, brine shrimp, mosquito larvae. Floating pellets accepted by acclimated specimens. Will ignore sinking foods. Small tankmates (Boraras, Microdevario, Pseudomugil fry) are at significant predation risk.

goodWith: medium-sized peaceful tetras (e.g., black skirt, bleeding heart)
danios and rasboras of similar body size
avoidWith: nano fish (Boraras, Microdevario, Danionella) — predation risk
Pseudomugil fry and other small surface-dwelling species
dwarf shrimp (Cherry shrimp, Amano shrimp) — will be picked off
long-finned or slow fish sharing the surface zone
conspecific males in insufficient space without cover (territorial and conspecific aggression escalates)

imageCandidates.notes: Clear lateral shot of adult male in typical coloration; good resolution. Photographer: Ranjit Laha.
FishBase species image; licensing may restrict reuse — verify before uploading.

summary: A hardy, wide-ranging surface killifish from South and Southeast Asia; not an annual — eggs hatch without diapause. Euryhaline, tolerating brackish conditions. An active jumper requiring a tight-fitting lid, and a predator of nano fish and small fry.
careNotes:
The Blue Panchax is one of the most widely distributed killifish in Asia, found from Pakistan and India east through Southeast Asia to Indonesia, inhabiting surface layers of rice paddies, ponds, ditches, swamps, and coastal brackish creeks. Unlike the annual killifish of the Nothobranchius genus, A. panchax is a non-annual species — eggs hatch normally within 10–14 days without a diapause dormancy period, which makes breeding considerably simpler.

Tank setup should prioritise dense floating plant cover (water lettuce, frogbit) and a tightly fitting lid — the Blue Panchax is an accomplished jumper that will exploit any gap, and escape risk is high. Keep the surface calm; strong surface agitation stresses the fish and disrupts their natural feeding posture. A 60 L tank of at least 60 cm length suits a small group of 3–6, with larger groups and mixed-sex keeping requiring proportionally more space.

The species is euryhaline: it tolerates a wide range of salinities from soft fresh water to light brackish (SG up to approximately 1.005). Many keepers report improved vitality and reduced disease incidence at very low brackish salinity (SG 1.001–1.003), reflecting its estuarine native habitat, though this is optional in captivity.

Water conditions are forgiving: pH 6.0–7.8, moderate hardness, and temperatures of 22–28°C are all broadly acceptable. The species is well-suited to beginners despite the 'killifish' label, which sometimes implies difficulty — Blue Panchax are robust, disease-tolerant, and eat readily.

Compatibility is the primary management concern: males of the same species display mild to moderate conspecific aggression, and the fish will eat anything small enough to fit in its mouth at the surface, including Boraras, Microdevario, nano rasboras, small fry, and dwarf shrimp. Only house with tankmates too large to be predated upon.
breedingNotes: Breeding is straightforward compared with annual killifish. The Blue Panchax is an egg-scatterer that deposits adhesive eggs in fine-leaved floating plants (Java moss, spawning mops, hornwort at the surface). Eggs hatch in approximately 10–14 days at 24–26°C — no diapause treatment is needed. Separate parents from eggs or provide a dedicated breeding tank with a spawning mop to prevent egg predation. Fry are free-swimming shortly after hatching and accept infusoria, vinegar eels, or micro-worm initially, transitioning to baby brine shrimp (BBS) and micro-pellets within a week or two. Spawning occurs regularly with little conditioning beyond live and frozen food feeding; conditioning with live daphnia and bloodworm enhances spawn size.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Aplocheilus panchax
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~8 cm), temperature range, pH, hardness, native range across South and Southeast Asia, biotope (rice paddies, ponds, ditches, brackish coastal waters), diet (surface insectivore), breeding mode (non-annual egg scatterer, 10–14 day hatch), and compatibility notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Aplocheilus panchax
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 8 cm TL), native distribution (South Asia through Indochina to Malay Archipelago), taxonomy (Aplocheilidae, Cyprinodontiformes), and euryhaline status. FishBase records distribution across India, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand, Malaysia, and Indonesia.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Killifish Care
additional.notes: General killifish guide covering Aplocheilus among non-annual species. Confirmed beginner suitability, surface predator behaviour, need for tight lid, and floating plant requirements. Noted that Aplocheilus panchax is among the hardiest and most recommended killifish for newcomers.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Panchax Killifish Profile
additional.notes: Confirmed size (to 8 cm), surface-dwelling behaviour, carnivorous diet focused on surface insects and larvae, tank requirements including tight lid and surface vegetation, and brackish tolerance. Also noted mild male-to-male aggression and predation on nano fish.

additional.name: Killifish Association — Aplocheilus panchax species account
additional.notes: Specialist killifish keeper account confirming non-annual status (eggs hatch in 10–14 days, no diapause), spawning behaviour in floating plants/mops, fry rearing approach (infusoria to BBS), and wide tolerance of water conditions. Noted the species' suitability as a first killifish.

fish.breedingNotes: Non-annual egg scatterer. Deposits adhesive eggs in floating plants or spawning mops; eggs hatch in ~10–14 days at 24–26°C with no diapause required. Fry start on infusoria/micro-worm, transitioning to BBS within days. No specialised setup required; remove eggs or parents to a separate vessel to prevent predation.
